items.prompt_md: In the trie above, what actually marks a node as the end of a stored word, as opposed to just being a waypoint along some longer word's path?
options: Whether the node has zero children
The `is_word` boolean stored on that node
Whether the node sits at the deepest level of the tree
Whether the node was the first one created for that word
items.answer: The `is_word` boolean stored on that node
items.explanation_md: A node partway through one word's path can simultaneously be the end of a shorter word — like the node for `an` sitting inside `ant`'s path — so only the flag reveals whether stopping here counts as a match, not the node's position or child count.

items.prompt_md: You insert `ant` right after inserting `an`. Why doesn't `insert` allocate three brand-new nodes for `ant` from scratch?
options: Because `insert` hashes the whole word first and skips anything already inserted
Because walking the path reuses whichever child nodes for `a` and `n` already exist, only creating a new node once the path diverges at `t`
Because the trie stores one global node per letter, shared across the whole alphabet
Because `an` and `ant` are stored in separate, identical-looking subtrees
items.answer: Because walking the path reuses whichever child nodes for `a` and `n` already exist, only creating a new node once the path diverges at `t`
items.explanation_md: `children.setdefault(ch, TrieNode())` hands back the existing child if one is already there, so the shared `a` then `n` steps are walked, not rebuilt — only the new `t` step actually allocates a node.

items.prompt_md: For a trie holding words up to length L, what is the time complexity of a single `insert` call?
options: O(L)
O(log L)
items.answer: O(L)
items.explanation_md: `insert` does exactly one dict lookup-or-create per character of the word, so its cost scales with that word's own length, not with anything else already stored in the trie.

items.prompt_md: Building a trie from scratch out of N words, each of length at most L, costs how much time in total?
options: O(N + L)
O(N * L)
O(N log N)
O(L^N)
items.answer: O(N * L)
items.explanation_md: Each of the N words costs O(L) to insert, and inserting one word doesn't affect the cost of inserting another, so the total build time is simply the product, O(N * L).

items.prompt_md: Why can't a plain hash set of whole words answer a prefix query like starts-with `an` without inspecting every stored word?
options: Hash sets can only store integers, not strings
A set only knows whole-string equality — it has no notion of one string being a piece of a longer one, so it must compare the prefix against every stored word
Hash sets refuse to contain strings that share the same first letters
Sets require the input to already be sorted before prefix checks work
items.answer: A set only knows whole-string equality — it has no notion of one string being a piece of a longer one, so it must compare the prefix against every stored word
items.explanation_md: Membership and prefix-matching are different questions; a set is built only to answer the first one in O(1), so the second one degrades to an O(N * L) scan of everything stored.

items.prompt_md: You're adding wildcard support: a `.` in the query should match any single letter. How should `search` change to handle a `.`?
options: Treat `.` as a literal character and look for a child node literally keyed by `.`
When the current query character is `.`, try every child of the current node (branch into each one) instead of following one fixed key
Replace every `.` with the letter `a` and continue as a normal search
Reject the query immediately, since wildcards can't be supported by a trie
items.answer: When the current query character is `.`, try every child of the current node (branch into each one) instead of following one fixed key
items.explanation_md: A `.` means any letter can appear here, so the walk can no longer follow a single dict lookup — it has to fan out into a small DFS across all of the node's children and succeed if any branch completes the rest of the query.

items.prompt_md: For a query of length L over an alphabet of size sigma, what is the worst-case time complexity of the DFS-based wildcard search if every character in the query happens to be a wildcard?
options: O(L)
O(sigma * L)
O(sigma^L)
O(L^sigma)
items.answer: O(sigma^L)
items.explanation_md: Each wildcard can branch into up to sigma children, and those branches compound across the length of the query, so an all-wildcard query is exponential in L rather than the O(L) of a wildcard-free search.

items.prompt_md: A teammate's `insert` correctly walks or creates a node for every character of a word but forgets to set `is_word = True` on the final node. What breaks?
options: Nothing — `search` will still find the word, since the path exists
`insert` crashes with a key error the next time that word is inserted
`search` on that exact word returns False even though every character's node exists, because `search` checks the end-of-word flag, not just reachability
`starts_with` on that word's own prefixes also starts failing
items.answer: `search` on that exact word returns False even though every character's node exists, because `search` checks the end-of-word flag, not just reachability
items.explanation_md: Reaching the final node only proves the letters were walked; without the flag that node looks like just a waypoint for some longer word, so `search` reports the word absent. `starts_with` is unaffected, since it only checks reachability.

items.prompt_md: What's the key difference between what `search` and `starts_with` check, given the same trie and the same input string?
options: There is no difference; they always return the same result
`search` additionally requires the final node's `is_word` flag to be set; `starts_with` only requires the path to exist
`starts_with` requires the flag to be set; `search` only requires the path to exist
`search` runs in O(1) while `starts_with` runs in O(L)
items.answer: `search` additionally requires the final node's `is_word` flag to be set; `starts_with` only requires the path to exist
items.explanation_md: Both walk the same characters; `starts_with` stops at whether the path exists, while `search` adds one more check — that the node landed on is actually marked as a complete word, not just a prefix of something longer.

items.prompt_md: In the worst case — no two words sharing any prefix at all — what is the space complexity of storing N words with maximum length L?
options: O(N + L)
O(N * L)
O(L)
O(N)
items.answer: O(N * L)
items.explanation_md: With zero sharing, every one of the N words contributes its own private chain of L nodes, so total node count scales with the product N * L; shared prefixes are exactly what pull real-world usage below that worst case.

items.prompt_md: The word `car` is already stored in a trie. You then insert `cart`. How many brand-new nodes does that second insert create?
options: 4, one for each letter of `cart`
1, just for the trailing `t`, since `c`, `a`, and `r` already exist along `car`'s path
0, because `cart` already contains `car`
3, one for each letter after the shared `c`
items.answer: 1, just for the trailing `t`, since `c`, `a`, and `r` already exist along `car`'s path
items.explanation_md: Insert reuses whatever prefix path already exists; only the point where the new word's letters diverge from every existing word requires a fresh node — here, that's just the final `t`.

items.prompt_md: Ignoring how many words are stored overall, what is the space cost of a single trie node, in terms of the alphabet size sigma?
options: O(1) — a node's size never depends on sigma
O(sigma), since its children map can hold at most one entry per possible next letter
O(sigma^2)
O(log sigma)
items.answer: O(sigma), since its children map can hold at most one entry per possible next letter
items.explanation_md: Each node's children dict has at most one entry per letter of the alphabet, so a node's own footprint is bounded by sigma — this is why alphabet size, not just word count, matters for a trie's real memory use.

items.prompt_md: Why is deleting a word from a trie trickier than just clearing that word's final `is_word` flag?
options: It isn't trickier — clearing the flag is always sufficient and safe
Because nodes along that word's path may be shared with other stored words, so removing them outright could delete parts of unrelated words too
Because tries don't support any form of deletion by design
Because clearing the flag requires re-inserting every other stored word
items.answer: Because nodes along that word's path may be shared with other stored words, so removing them outright could delete parts of unrelated words too
items.explanation_md: Clearing the flag alone is safe and sufficient for correctness, but if you also want to free now-unused nodes, each one must be checked for other children or another word's ending before it's removed.

items.prompt_md: You're building an autocomplete box: as a user types each letter, you need to instantly know whether any stored word starts with what they've typed so far, across a large and growing word list. Which approach fits?
options: Keep a hash set of whole words and rescan every one of them on each keystroke
Sort the word list and binary search for the typed prefix on each keystroke
Build a prefix tree (trie) keyed by character, and walk it one letter deeper as the user types
Compare the typed text against every word using string equality
items.answer: Build a prefix tree (trie) keyed by character, and walk it one letter deeper as the user types
items.explanation_md: Autocomplete is a growing sequence of prefix queries against the same dictionary — the classic trie trigger — and each keystroke only extends the walk already done for the previous one, staying O(L) per keystroke.

items.prompt_md: You need a word-search API that treats `.` as a wildcard matching any single letter, checked against a dictionary of stored words. Which approach fits?
options: A hash set of whole words, expanding every possible wildcard combination before checking membership
A trie, doing a DFS at each `.` that branches into every child of the current node
Sort the words and binary search using only the non-wildcard characters
Scan the dictionary linearly, comparing each stored word to the pattern character by character
items.answer: A trie, doing a DFS at each `.` that branches into every child of the current node
items.explanation_md: Wildcard matching against a shared dictionary is a trie's branching-DFS use case; the structure lets a `.` fan out into all children at that node instead of forcing a full rescan of every word.

items.prompt_md: You just need to check, once, whether the exact word pineapple is present in a dictionary of 50,000 words — no prefixes, no wildcards, nothing repeated. Which approach fits?
options: Build a trie and walk it letter by letter to confirm the word ends exactly there
Put the words in a hash set and check membership directly
Sort the dictionary and binary search for the word
Scan every word manually, comparing character by character
items.answer: Put the words in a hash set and check membership directly
items.explanation_md: A single exact-membership check, with no prefix or wildcard behavior ever needed, is exactly what a hash set answers in O(1) average time — a trie's extra structure buys nothing here.

items.prompt_md: You're building a boggle-style word-search game: while exploring the board letter by letter, you want to abandon a path the instant no dictionary word could possibly continue with those letters. Which approach fits?
options: Precompute a hash set of all valid words and check the board path against it only once it's fully formed
Build a trie of the dictionary and, as you extend the board path, check whether it's still a valid prefix in the trie — pruning the moment it isn't
Sort the dictionary and binary search after adding each new letter to the path
Store the dictionary in a list and rescan all of it after finishing each board path
items.answer: Build a trie of the dictionary and, as you extend the board path, check whether it's still a valid prefix in the trie — pruning the moment it isn't
items.explanation_md: Needing to know whether a partial path is still a prefix of some word, at every single step, is a trie's `starts_with` check used for pruning — a set can only confirm whole words, too late to prune early.

items.prompt_md: You need to implement network-style longest-prefix-match: given an address, find the longest of several stored prefixes that matches its start. Which approach fits?
options: A hash set of all stored prefixes, checked one at a time for exact equality
A trie built over the address characters, walking down while nodes remain valid prefix-ends and remembering the deepest match seen
Sort all stored prefixes and take the numerically smallest
Scan a flat list of prefixes comparing their string lengths
items.answer: A trie built over the address characters, walking down while nodes remain valid prefix-ends and remembering the deepest match seen
items.explanation_md: Longest-prefix-match is inherently about walking shared prefix structure and tracking the deepest valid stopping point along the way — precisely what a trie is built to do.

items.prompt_md: You need to remove duplicate words from a list before displaying it, and the order of the output doesn't matter. Which approach fits?
options: Build a trie of all the words and read back one word per root-to-leaf path
Convert the list to a set and back to a list
Sort the list and merge adjacent duplicates
Compare every pair of words with nested loops
items.answer: Convert the list to a set and back to a list
items.explanation_md: Deduplication with no ordering requirement and no prefix behavior is a plain set operation — reaching for a trie here would add structure the problem never actually asked for.

items.prompt_md: Given a dictionary of words, you need to repeatedly answer: how many stored words begin with this prefix, for many different prefixes. Which approach fits?
options: A hash set of whole words, filtered with a linear scan per query
A trie where each node also stores a count of how many words pass through it, so a prefix query is a single walk-and-read
Sort the words alphabetically and binary search for the prefix's range boundaries every time
A hash map from each whole word to 1, summed per query
items.answer: A trie where each node also stores a count of how many words pass through it, so a prefix query is a single walk-and-read
items.explanation_md: Repeated 'how many words share this prefix' queries are answered in O(L) by annotating trie nodes with a pass-through counter during insert, instead of rescanning the whole dictionary per query.

items.prompt_md: The problem statement literally asks you to implement a class with `insert(word)`, `search(word)`, and `startsWith(prefix)` methods over a dictionary of words. Which approach fits?
options: A hash map from each word to its length
A trie (prefix tree), one node per character along each word's path, with an end-of-word flag
A sorted array with binary search backing each method
A doubly linked list of words in insertion order
items.answer: A trie (prefix tree), one node per character along each word's path, with an end-of-word flag
items.explanation_md: This is the trie interface by name — `insert` / `search` / `startsWith` together are the textbook signal to reach directly for a prefix tree rather than reconstruct the idea on top of a hash set.

items.prompt_md: You're building a spelling-suggestion feature: given a partial prefix someone has typed, list every dictionary word that shares it, to offer as completions. Which approach fits?
options: A trie: walk down to the prefix's node, then DFS the subtree beneath it collecting every word found
A hash set of whole words, checked one at a time against the prefix using string slicing
Sort the dictionary and binary search only for an exact match of the prefix
A stack that pops words in reverse insertion order
items.answer: A trie: walk down to the prefix's node, then DFS the subtree beneath it collecting every word found
items.explanation_md: Listing every completion of a shared prefix is a trie's core trick: reach the prefix's node once in O(L), then every word sharing that prefix lives in the subtree below it, ready to be collected.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of strings, you need their longest common prefix — the longest string that is a prefix of every one of them. Which approach fits?
options: Put all strings in a hash set and check pairwise equality between them
Build a trie of all the strings, then walk down from the root while each node has exactly one child and is shared by every string
Reverse each string and compare their suffixes instead
Count character frequencies across all the strings
items.answer: Build a trie of all the strings, then walk down from the root while each node has exactly one child and is shared by every string
items.explanation_md: The longest common prefix is exactly the single shared path from the root before the trie's paths first branch or a word ends — turning the problem into a walk over shared structure instead of pairwise string comparison.

items.prompt_md: You need to check, for thousands of independent lookups, whether each of several complete usernames is already taken against a fixed registered-usernames list — no partial matches are ever needed. Which approach fits?
options: Build a trie of all registered usernames and call `search` for each lookup
Put all registered usernames in a hash set and check membership for each lookup
Sort the usernames and binary search for each one
Scan the full username list for every lookup
items.answer: Put all registered usernames in a hash set and check membership for each lookup
items.explanation_md: Even with many repeated lookups, if every one is exact whole-string membership and prefixes never matter, a hash set gives the same O(1) average answer with less overhead than a trie's per-character structure.

items.prompt_md: You're given a set of words and need to determine whether any word is a prefix of another word in the same set (for example, be is a prefix of bee). Which approach fits?
options: Insert all words into a trie and, during each insert, check whether you pass through an existing end-of-word node, or finish on a node that already has children
Put all words into a hash set and check pairwise string equality
Sort the words by length and compare only adjacent pairs
Reverse every word and look for matching suffixes
items.answer: Insert all words into a trie and, during each insert, check whether you pass through an existing end-of-word node, or finish on a node that already has children
items.explanation_md: Detecting a prefix relationship between stored words is a direct trie signal: while inserting, passing through an already-flagged word node, or ending on a node that already has children, both reveal a prefix relationship in O(L) per word instead of comparing every pair.
